request.question: Are the Sternbergia and Echinopsis genuses of plants both native to the same regions?

title: Sternbergia vernalis
Echinopsis backebergii
Sternbergia
Sternbergia colchiciflora
Echinopsis scopulicola
Narcisseae
Sternbergia pulchella
Echinopsis
Echinopsis pachanoi
Echinopsis peruviana
sentences: Sternbergia vernalis is a bulbous flowering plant in the family Amaryllidaceae, subfamily Amaryllidoideae, which is sometimes used as an ornamental plant.
It has yellow flowers which appear in spring.
The species is native to central and southwestern Asia (Turkmenistan, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Georgia, Iran, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria and Turkey).
Echinopsis backebergii is a species of flowering plant in the cactus family Cactaceae, native to eastern Bolivia and southern Peru.
It grows to 5 cm tall and wide, with single or clustered globose stems 4-5 cm thick, with about 15 ribs and covered in grey-brown spines.
Large, showy, carmine-red flowers are borne in summer.
As the minimum temperature requirement is 10 C , in temperate regions it must be grown under glass with heat.
Sternbergia is a genus of Eurasian and North African plants in the Amaryllis family, subfamily Amaryllidoideae.
Sternbergia colchiciflora is a bulbous flowering plant in the family Amaryllidaceae, subfamily Amaryllidoideae, which is sometimes used as an ornamental plant.
The species is native to southern Europe from Spain to Ukraine, as well as from Morocco, Algeria, Turkey, Syria, Lebanon, Palestine, Iran and the Caucasus.
It has yellow flowers which appear in autumn.
Echinopsis scopulicola is a type of cactus native to Bolivia, which contains psychedelic compounds.

Many claim this species contains mescaline, which was until recently unconfirmed due to the lack of official research, but a study published in September 2010 by the International Society for Ethnopharmacology placed it in the top third of the results for their samples.
This included 14 taxa/cultivars of the subgenus Trichocereus of the genus Echinopsis, with Scopulicola noted to contain 0.82% mescaline/dry weight in the outer green layer.

Narcisseae is a small tribe of plants belonging to the subfamily Amaryllidoideae of the Amaryllis family (Amaryllidaceae), where it forms part of the Eurasian clade, and is one of three tribes in the European (Mediterranean) clade.
It contains two genera ("Narcissus" and "Sternbergia") and approximately 58 species, but probably also "Lapiedra".
The two genera are distinguished from each other by the presence of a paraperigonium in the former.
Sternbergia pulchella is a bulbous flowering plant in the family Amaryllidaceae, subfamily Amaryllidoideae.
It has yellow flowers which appear in autumn.
The species is native to Syria and Lebanon.
Echinopsis is a large genus of cacti native to South America, sometimes known as hedgehog cactus, sea-urchin cactus or Easter lily cactus.
One small species, "E. chamaecereus", is known as the peanut cactus.
The 128 species range from large and treelike types to small globose cacti.
The name derives from "echinos" hedgehog or sea urchin, and "opsis" appearance, a reference to these plants' dense coverings of spines.
Echinopsis pachanoi (syn.
Trichocereus pachanoi) — known as San Pedro cactus — is a fast-growing columnar cactus native to the Andes Mountains at 2000 – in altitude.
It is found in Argentina, Bolivia, Chile, Ecuador and Peru, and it is cultivated in other parts of the world.
Uses for it include traditional medicine and traditional veterinary medicine, and it is widely grown as an ornamental cactus.
It has been used for healing and religious divination in the Andes Mountains region for over 3,000 years.
It is sometimes confused with its close relative "Echinopsis peruviana" (Peruvian torch cactus).
Echinopsis peruviana (syn.
Trichocereus peruvianus), Peruvian torch cactus, is a fast-growing columnar cactus native to the western slope of the Andes in Peru, between about 2000 - above sea level.
It contains the psychoactive alkaloid mescaline as well as other alkaloids, although reported levels vary considerably and do not approach the concentrations found in "Echinopsis pachanoi".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Are the Sternbergia and Echinopsis genuses of plants both native to the same regions?
hop1_docs: Sternbergia | Sternbergia is a genus of Eurasian and North African plants in the Amaryllis family, subfamily Amaryllidoideae.

Echinopsis chiloensis | Echinopsis chiloensis is a species of cacti native to South America; genus members are known as "hedgehog cacti", "sea-urchin cactus" or "Easter lily cactus".  This species, "E chiloensis", is tree-like with branching structures beginning basally.  This plant can attain a height of eight metres.  An example occurrence is within the La Campana National Park in Chile.

Sternbergia pulchella | Sternbergia pulchella is a bulbous flowering plant in the family Amaryllidaceae, subfamily Amaryllidoideae.  It has yellow flowers which appear in autumn.  The species is native to Syria and Lebanon.

Sternbergia vernalis | Sternbergia vernalis is a bulbous flowering plant in the family Amaryllidaceae, subfamily Amaryllidoideae, which is sometimes used as an ornamental plant.  It has yellow flowers which appear in spring.  The species is native to central and southwestern Asia (Turkmenistan, Tajikistan, Uzbekistan, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Georgia, Iran, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria and Turkey).

Sternbergia colchiciflora | Sternbergia colchiciflora is a bulbous flowering plant in the family Amaryllidaceae, subfamily Amaryllidoideae, which is sometimes used as an ornamental plant.  The species is native to southern Europe from Spain to Ukraine, as well as from Morocco, Algeria, Turkey, Syria, Lebanon, Palestine, Iran and the Caucasus.  It has yellow flowers which appear in autumn.

Echinopsis | Echinopsis is a large genus of cacti native to South America, sometimes known as hedgehog cactus, sea-urchin cactus or Easter lily cactus.  One small species, "E. chamaecereus", is known as the peanut cactus.  The 128 species range from large and treelike types to small globose cacti.  The name derives from "echinos" hedgehog or sea urchin, and "opsis" appearance, a reference to these plants' dense coverings of spines.

Echinopsis backebergii | Echinopsis backebergii is a species of flowering plant in the cactus family Cactaceae, native to eastern Bolivia and southern Peru.  It grows to 5 cm tall and wide, with single or clustered globose stems 4-5 cm thick, with about 15 ribs and covered in grey-brown spines.  Large, showy, carmine-red flowers are borne in summer.  As the minimum temperature requirement is 10 C , in temperate regions it must be grown under glass with heat.
response.summary_1: Evidence: "Sternbergia is a genus of Eurasian and North African plants" (passage 1); species-level supports: Sternbergia species are "native to Syria and Lebanon" (passage 3), "native to central and southwestern Asia" (passage 4), and "native to southern Europe ... Morocco, Algeria" (passage 5). Evidence: "Echinopsis is a large genus of cacti native to South America" (passage 6); species-level examples: Echinopsis chiloensis "is a species of cacti native to South America" (passage 2) and Echinopsis backebergii is "native to eastern Bolivia and southern Peru" (passage 7). Missing/bridge fact for next hop: any authoritative source showing either genus is native to the other's region (i.e., Sternbergia native to South America or Echinopsis native to Eurasia/North Africa) — this is not present in the passages.
response.hop2_query: Sternbergia native to South America OR Echinopsis native to Eurasia North Africa
hop2_docs: Gamochaeta pensylvanica | Gamochaeta pensylvanica, called Pennsylvania cudweed or Pennsylvania everlasting, is a widespread species of flowering plant in the sunflower family.  It is native to South America and introduced into Eurasia, Africa, Australia, and North America.  The "pensylvanica" epithet is a misnomer, as the plant is not native to Pennsylvania and only marginally naturalized there.

Eragrostis pilosa | Eragrostis pilosa is a species of grass.  It is native to Eurasia and Africa.  It may or may not be native to North America.  It is widely introduced, and it is a common weed in many areas.

Echinopsis terscheckii | Echinopsis terscheckii, commonly known as the cardon grande cactus or Argentine saguaro, is a large cactus native to South America and popular in cultivation.  It is a columnar, branching cactus that can grow over 7.6 m tall.
Cnidium | Cnidium is a genus of flowering plant in the Apiaceae, native to Eurasia, Africa and North America.  It has 4 or 5 species.

Acanthospermum hispidum | Acanthospermum hispidum (bristly starbur, goat's head, hispid starburr, starbur) is an annual plant in the family Asteraceae, which is native to Central and South America.  This plant is cited as a weed in cotton culture in Brazil, and it is also used as a medicinal plant.  It is also naturalized in many scattered places in Eurasia, Africa, North America, etc.

Torilis | Torilis is a genus of plants which are known generally as the hedge parsleys.  They are native to Eurasia and North Africa but have been introduced to other continents.  "T. arvensis" is quite widespread in North America but is facing population decline in the UK.

Isolepis cernua | Isolepis cernua (syn.  "Scirpus cernuus") is a species of flowering plant in the sedge family known by the common names low bulrush, slender club-rush, tufted clubrush, and fiberoptic grass.  It is widespread, being native to many regions of the world, including parts of Australasia, Eurasia, Africa, and North and South America.
response.summary_2: No — Sternbergia is native to Eurasia and North Africa, while Echinopsis is native to South America; the passages include an Echinopsis species noted as South American but contain no evidence that either genus is native to the other's region.
